concept.text: A long, long time ago, people lived in caves! They made fire to stay warm and drew pictures on the walls.
concept.funFact: The oldest cave paintings are over 40,000 years old!

questions.prompt: Did cave people live in tall buildings?
What did cave people use to stay warm?

concept.text: In Egypt, people built giant triangle buildings called pyramids. They are huge!
concept.funFact: Pyramids took many, many years to build!

questions.prompt: What shape is a pyramid?
Are pyramids tiny?
Where are the famous pyramids?

concept.text: Knights wore shiny metal armor and lived in big stone castles with tall towers.
concept.funFact: Some castles had a moat — a ring of water around them!

questions.prompt: What did knights wear?
Where did knights live?

concept.text: Long ago, people sailed across the seas in big wooden ships to find new lands.
concept.funFact: Sailors used the stars to find their way!

questions.prompt: What did old ships use to move?
Were old ships made of wood?

concept.text: A very long time ago, people invented the wheel! It helped them move heavy things and travel far.
concept.funFact: Without the wheel, we wouldn't have cars or bikes today!

questions.prompt: What helps things roll?

concept.text: Ancient Egyptians lived along the Nile River over 5,000 years ago. They built pyramids as tombs for their pharaohs and wrote with picture symbols called hieroglyphs.
concept.funFact: The Great Pyramid of Giza weighs about 6 million tons!

questions.prompt: What river did Egyptians live by?

concept.text: Rome started as a small city in Italy and grew into a giant empire. The Romans built straight roads, huge arenas like the Colosseum, and aqueducts to carry water.
concept.funFact: Many Roman roads are still used today, 2,000 years later!

concept.text: In medieval times, kings and queens ruled lands from stone castles. Brave knights served them, and people lived in villages and worked the land.
concept.funFact: A knight's armor could weigh over 60 pounds!

concept.text: Thomas Edison created the light bulb. The Wright brothers built the first airplane. Inventors change the world by solving problems with new ideas.
concept.funFact: Edison tried thousands of materials before finding one that worked for the light bulb!

questions.prompt: Who invented the light bulb?

concept.text: Hundreds of years ago, explorers like Christopher Columbus and Ferdinand Magellan sailed across oceans in wooden ships to discover new lands and routes.
concept.funFact: Magellan's crew was the first to sail all the way around the world!

concept.text: The earliest civilizations rose in fertile river valleys: Mesopotamia (Tigris & Euphrates), Egypt (Nile), the Indus Valley, and ancient China (Yellow River). They invented writing, agriculture, and cities.
concept.funFact: Cuneiform, from Mesopotamia, is one of the world's oldest writing systems.

questions.prompt: Which rivers bordered Mesopotamia?
Cuneiform writing was developed in...
Ancient China's first major civilization grew along the ____ River.
Civilizations needed fertile land to grow.
Order from earliest to latest: Roman Empire, Sumerians, Industrial Revolution.

concept.text: World War I (1914–1918) and World War II (1939–1945) reshaped the modern world. They led to new technology, the United Nations, and major changes in global politics.
concept.funFact: The United Nations was formed in 1945 to help prevent future world wars.

questions.prompt: When did World War II end?
The United Nations was created after WWII.
WWI began in...
The military alliance opposed to the Axis powers was called the ____.

concept.text: The Renaissance (14th–17th century) was a rebirth of art, science, and ideas in Europe. Leonardo da Vinci painted the Mona Lisa and designed flying machines. Galileo studied the stars.
concept.funFact: Leonardo da Vinci kept thousands of pages of notes, often writing in mirror script.

questions.prompt: Who painted the Mona Lisa?
Galileo studied the stars and planets.
The Renaissance began in ____.

concept.text: Beginning in the late 1700s in Britain, the Industrial Revolution introduced steam engines, factories, and railways. It transformed how people worked, lived, and traveled.
concept.funFact: James Watt's improved steam engine in 1769 powered the new industrial age.

questions.prompt: Where did the Industrial Revolution begin?
The ____ engine powered factories and trains.
The Industrial Revolution moved many people from farms to cities.
Which is NOT a product of the Industrial Revolution?

concept.text: In the mid-20th century, leaders like Martin Luther King Jr. and Rosa Parks fought peacefully for equal rights for Black Americans. Their work changed laws and lives.
concept.funFact: Dr. King's 'I Have a Dream' speech was delivered in 1963 in Washington, D.C.
